title: - SCALING LAWS FOR NEURAL MACHINE TRANSLA TION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - SCALING LAWS FOR NEURAL MACHINE TRANSLA TION

## **Behrooz Ghorbani** _[∗]_ **, Orhan Firat, Markus Freitag, Ankur Bapna, Maxim Krikun, Xavier Garcia, Ciprian Chelba & Colin Cherry** Google AI

## ABSTRACT

We present an empirical study of the scaling properties of encoder-decoder Transformer models used in neural machine translation (NMT). We show that crossentropy loss as a function of model size follows a certain scaling law. Specifically (i) We propose a formula which describes the scaling behavior of cross-entropy loss as a bivariate function of encoder and decoder size, and show that it gives accurate predictions under a variety of scaling approaches and languages; we show that the total number of parameters alone is not sufficient for such purposes. (ii) We observe different power law exponents when scaling the decoder vs the encoder, and provide recommendations for optimal allocation of encoder/decoder capacity based on this observation. (iii) We also report that the scaling behavior of the model is acutely influenced by _composition bias_ of the train/test sets, which we define as any deviation from naturally generated text (either via machine generated or human translated text). We observe that natural text on the target side enjoys scaling, which manifests as successful reduction of the cross-entropy loss. (iv) Finally, we investigate the relationship between the model size, cross-entropy loss and the quality of the generated translations. We find two different behaviors, depending on the nature of the test data. For test sets which were originally translated from target language to source language, both loss and BLEU score improve as model size increases. In contrast, for test sets originally translated from source language to target language, the loss improves, but the BLEU score stops improving after a certain threshold. We release generated text from all models used in this study.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Scaling properties of neural networks have long been an intriguing topic of study (Ahmad & Tesauro, 1988; Amari et al., 1992). Along with the practical success of modern neural networks at scale, theoretical understanding of the factors governing the quality and training dynamics of large neural networks has also being developing (Advani et al., 2020; Rosenfeld et al., 2019; Geiger et al., 2020; Ghorbani et al., 2021; Cho, 2020; Hutter, 2021; Bahri et al., 2021; Loureiro et al., 2021). In particular, scaling model sizes, datasets and the total computation budget has been identified as a reliable approach to improve generalization performance on several machine learning tasks. For many of these tasks the scaling behavior of neural networks is highly predictable; model fit or test loss can be described precisely as a function of its number of parameters (Hestness et al., 2017; Kaplan et al., 2020; Henighan et al., 2020; Hernandez et al., 2021; Rosenfeld et al., 2019). Neural machine translation (NMT) has long enjoyed the benefits of scaling (Huang et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Lepikhin et al., 2021), but studies investigating the scaling behavior of NMT models are missing. We present a large-scale systematic study of scaling laws for encoder-decoder Transformer models applied to NMT (Vaswani et al., 2017).

We start with highlighting the major differences between decoder-only language models, where the majority of the previous work has focused, and encoder-decoder (conditional) language models applied to NMT. The two differ along a few crucial dimensions. The first difference results from the very nature of the separate architectures being used, i.e. decoder-only vs encoder-decoder. The presence of separate architectural components complicates the study of scaling properties due to the

> _∗_ Correspondence to ghorbani@google.com.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

increased degree of freedom. Second, contrary to language modeling, the task of machine translation is conditional: the task is predictive rather than fully generative. Furthermore, this prediction task is ambiguous: there is no one right answer for a given source, and translations can vary substantially depending on the translator’s incentives. This manifests itself as different scaling benefits for different test sets. To take an extreme example, a test set translated by someone who writes nearly wordfor-word translations may benefit less from model scaling than one translated by someone who considers each translation a work of art. In this work, these differences in difficulty coincide with the translation direction of the test set; that is, whether the source was translated into the target (source-original) or vice versa (target-original). Source-original data has translated text on the target side, which contains several artifacts of “translationese” that distinguish it from text originally written in that language, often lacking the diversity and complexity of “natural” text (Koppel & Ordan, 2011), while target-original data requires the prediction of more complex natural text on the target side. Finally, unlike language models, NMT is evaluated on metrics that quantify generation quality against reference translations (for eg. BLEU) (Papineni et al., 2002) instead of evaluating model fit (perplexity) on an evaluation set.

In this paper, we aim to provide empirical answers to the following research questions:

1. **Does the encoder-decoder architecture for NMT share the same scaling law function as language models?** Contrary to previous work on LM, we show that a univariate law depending on the total number of parameters in the network does not adequately describe the scaling behavior of NMT models. Our scaling laws parameterize the cross entropy loss as a bivariate function of the number of encoder parameters and the number of decoder parameters as separate variables. Our results indicate that the scaling behavior is largely determined by the total capacity of the model, and the capacity allocation between the encoder and the decoder.

2. **How does the naturalness of source/target side data affect scaling behavior?** We study the effect of naturalness of the source and the target text, both for training and evaluation. When evaluating with target side natural text, scaling the model capacity continues improving model quality throughout our range of measurements. On the other hand, improvements on cross-entropy saturate (or reaches the irreducible error region) on source side natural evaluation sets even for moderately-sized models.

3. **Do scaling improvements in cross-entropy translate into corresponding improvements in generation quality?** Finally we study the relationship between generation quality and cross-entropy and how their correlation changes as we: (i) Scale different components of the model (encoder vs decoder) and (ii) Evaluate on source-natural or target-natural test sets.

Our results on multiple language pairs and training/test data compositions validate that **model scaling predictably improves the cross-entropy on validation data** . However, our findings also raise several questions regarding the effect of naturalness of training and evaluation text and how cross-entropy eventually relates with generation quality for auto-regressive generative models.[1]

- 2 EFFECT OF SCALING ON CROSS-ENTROPY

- 2.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTING

**Model Architectures and Training** We train a series of pre-layer norm Transformer networks with varying sizes (Xiong et al., 2020). Models are trained with per-token cross-entropy loss and Adafactor optimizer (Shazeer & Stern, 2018). All models are trained with a fixed batch-size of 500k tokens and dropout rate of 0 _._ 1 for residuals, feed-forward activations and attention. All models are trained to near convergence for 500k training steps. Details of the model hyper-parameters are described in Appendix C.
**Model Scaling** Transformer architecture consists of Transformer Blocks: a cascade of self-attention, cross-attention and feed-forward layers, each having multiple adjustable hyper-parameters (e.g.

> 1A few weeks before the publication of this manuscript on Arxiv, Gordon et al. (2021) appeared on OpenReview. While both papers study scaling laws for NMT, our studies focus on different parameter regimes (393K-56M vs 100M-4B). See Appendix A for a comparison of the results.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

model-dimension, number of attention heads, attention projection dimension etc.). Considering the combinatorial expansion of the search space for scaling each one, in this study we choose to vary only the total number of Transformer Blocks, while keeping the internal hyper-parameters intact across different scales. In other words, we scale the depth of the Transformers while keeping width and other variables fixed.[2] We use GPipe pipeline parallelism for our experiments (Huang et al., 2019).

In an encoder-decoder Transformer architecture for NMT, depth scaling can naturally be implemented by varying encoder-decoder blocks independently or symmetrically. Hence, we examine the change in the cross-entropy loss as the number of parameters increase with three depth scaling approaches:

_Encoder Scaling_ : vary encoder depth (2 to 64) while decoder depth is fixed (6 layers).

_Decoder Scaling_ : vary decoder depth (2 to 64) while encoder depth is fixed (6 layers).
_Symmetric Scaling_ : increasing decoder and encoder layers together (from 2 to 64), i.e. the number of Transformer Blocks in the encoder and decoder being equal.

For all experiments, configuration of the individual layers is unchanged: the model dimension, width of the feed-forward layer, and number of attention heads are fixed respectively at 1024, 8192, and 16. 3 Each encoder layer adds approximately 20M parameters to the model while each decoder layer adds around 25M parameters. We train 200 such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of 50 (from 80M parameters to 4B parameters). Following the convention, we do not count the parameters in the embedding and softmax layers towards the model size. We focus our study on large-scale models: our smallest models require 200 TPUv3 days to train to convergence while our largest models require 2700 TPUv3 days of training.

**Language Pairs** We report results on four language pairs: English _→_ German, German _→_ English, English _→_ Chinese, and Chinese _→_ English. We use in-house web-crawled training datasets with around 2 _._ 2 billion sentence pairs (approximately 55 billion tokens) for English _↔_ German and 781 million sentence pairs for English _↔_ Chinese.

**Evaluation Sets** We use a variety of test sets for evaluation covering different domains: (i) WebDomain (ii) News-Domain (iii) Wikipedia (iv) Patents. The news-domain test sets come from the WMT2019 (Barrault et al., 2019) evaluation campaign (newstest2019) for all language pairs. The other test sets are internal test sets representing the different domains, ranging from 500 to 5000 sentence pairs. For each domain, we randomly sample sentences in the source language and use professional translators to generate a reference translation in the target language. Throughout the paper, we will refer this type of test sets as _source-original_ as the source sentences have been crawled from the web while the reference translations are added later. For most of the domains, we also have a _target-original_ counterpart which is generated in the opposite direction: Sentences are crawled in the target language and human translated into the source language. Earlier work (Freitag et al., 2019; 2020; Graham et al., 2020) showed that it is important to differentiate between the two different kinds of test sets as the style of natural sentences and human (or machine) translations (translationese) is quite different. Cross-entropy loss is evaluated on the different test sets during training. To reduce the variation caused by the parameter fluctuations at the end of the training, we present the median loss over the last 50k steps of the training as the final loss.

## 2.2 RESULTS

Figure 1 shows the empirical evolution of the test loss on the Web-Domain test sets for encoder and decoder scaling for English _→_ German. To compare the empirical results with the scaling laws present in the literature for decoder only models (Kaplan et al., 2020; Henighan et al., 2020), we have fitted a power law of the form _L_[ˆ] ( _N_ ) = _αN[−][p]_ + _L∞_ to the data.[4] Here, _N_ is the total number of parameters outside of embedding / softmax layers and _{α, p, L∞}_ are fitted parameters. As Figure 1 suggests, scaling the encoder has different effects on the test loss compared to scaling the decoder. As such, simple power-laws that only consider the total parameter count, fail to capture the correct scaling behavior of the model.

As such, traditional scaling laws are unable to capture the correct scaling behavior. R-squared (100 _×_[ex][p] total variance[lained variance] ) and maximum absolute deviation ( _∥· ∥∞_ ) are reported for each fit.

**Proposed Scaling Law** To tackle this issue, we present a new scaling law that reflects the encoderdecoder nature of the architecture as well as the bilingual format of the data. Let _Ne_ and _Nd_ be the number of non-embedding parameters in the encoder and the decoder respectively. Then, our proposed scaling law has the form

where _{α, pe, pd, L∞}_ are test set specific (fitted) parameters. In this formulation, _α_ corresponds to the maximum loss reduction that one can hope from scaling, while _pe_ and _pd_ are the scaling exponents for encoder and decoder respectively. _L∞_ corresponds to the irreducible loss of the data.

In Figure 2, we fit our scaling law to the empirical loss values achieved by encoder/decoder scaling models on Web-Domain test sets. The results suggest that our scaling law almost fully captures the variation in the data ( _R_[2] _>_ 99%). In particular, in contrast to traditional scaling laws, our proposed formulation is able to capture the different scaling behaviors of encoder and decoder scaled models.

While these improved fits are encouraging, to truly show that a candidate scaling law captures the model scaling behavior in NMT, it is necessary to verify the out-of-sample prediction power of the law. We provide two such verifications; we compare the predictions of the scaling law fitted on encoder / decoder scaling models with the empirical loss values from (i) symmetrically scaled models, (ii) models randomly sampled from the 2D grid of all potential encoder-decoder configurations.[5] Symmetrically scaled models are chosen for this verification as they are widely used by the NLP community (Raffel et al., 2020). Randomly sampled models are chosen to ensure that our results are not biased towards any particular model class.

Figures 3 & 4 present the out-of-sample results. As the plots suggest, the predictions of the scaling law match the empirical results with remarkable accuracy. These results suggest that Eq. (1) is able to capture the model scaling behavior across different scaling approaches and model sizes.

To ensure that our results generalize across different language pairs, we examine the fit of our scaling law on encoder / decoder scaling models trained on German _→_ English (De _→_ En), Chinese-to-English (Zh _→_ En), and English-to-Chinese (En _→_ Zh) translation tasks. Figure 5 presents the results evaluated on in-domain held-out data. Similar to the En _→_ De case, our proposed functional form is able to closely capture the scaling behavior of these models.

To keep the scope of our experiments manageable, we focus on depth scaling in our analysis. In Appendix H, we ran large-scale experiments to see if other scaling approaches show significant deviations from the trends identified here. For large-scale models, which are the focus of this study, we observe that different scaling approaches yield very similar results: the final achieved test loss across different scaling approaches shows just 1 _−_ 2% variation. We observed that for small models ( _<_ 100M parameters), this variation tends to grow, suggesting that model shape plays a key role in the performance of small models. We leave further analysis of this phenomenon to future research.

## 2.3 ANALYSIS

The above results suggest that the scaling law formalized in Eq. (1) captures the scaling behavior of the Transformer NMT models in multiple language pairs. As such, we can study the fitted coefficients to fully understand the scaling properties of these models. Several observations are in order:

**Decoder vs Encoder Scaling:** On all our test sets, the decoder exponents were observed to be larger than the encoder exponents, _pd > pe_ (see Figure 9). As a result, when improving the test loss is concerned, it is much more effective to scale the decoder rather than the encoder. This is contrary to the usual practice; due to latency considerations, many practitioners train NMT models with deep encoders and shallow decoders (Kasai et al., 2021). Our results suggest this practice could be sub-optimal in terms of loss reduction. Proposition 1 below provides guidance on how to allocate parameters in between the encoder and decoder optimally. ## 3 EFFECT OF DATASET COMPOSITION BIAS ON SCALING BEHAVIOR

Translation deals with the problem of mapping a sequence in one language into another language. A good translation should not only be adequate and fluent, but should ideally also adopt the style of a sentence naturally written in the target language. This necessitates MT models to make sense of natural looking inputs and generate natural looking outputs. As mentioned in Section 2, the examples used to train or test NMT models carry a critical bias, which we refer to as _composition bias_ . Composition bias is introduced because of the unavailability of source-target examples (pairs) that are both natural in the accessible data generating distribution. For any given naturally generated text in a language, the corresponding text in the other language is either translated by humans, introducing _translationese_ bias or translated by other machine translation systems, introducing _MT_ bias. We consider both biases affecting the problem from a similar angle, hence we bundle them and call it composition bias. While machine translation by design has composition bias in the training/test sets employed (Freitag et al., 2020; Riley et al., 2020), its effect on model scaling is unknown. In this section we investigate the role of composition bias in scaling and identify critical factors playing a role.

We caution the reader to not take the composition bias as a problem specific to NMT. In fact as most training corpora in NMT are web-crawled, they can contain machine translation output on either the source or target side. Considering the growth of generated content in the web by machine learning

6

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

models[6][7] , it is not improbable that a proportion of the content collected and used by machine learning models is going to be biased by other models that are continuously generating content.

**The Effect of Test Set Construction:** We first examine the impact of composition bias on the test sets. Figure 9 shows the fitted scaling exponents for all our test sets. The plot suggests that the scaling powers for source-original test sets are drastically different from those of target-original test sets. This behavior is in direct contrast with language modeling setting (Kaplan et al., 2020) where it was observed that the evaluation on different test sets merely acted as a scaling penalty that only changed the multiplicative constants of the scaling law.

To elucidate this phenomenon further, in Figure 11 of the appendix, we compare the scaling trends for different source and target original test sets. Several observations are in order: Test sets with a similar composition approach (source or target original) have a qualitatively similar scaling behavior. However, scaling behavior is vastly different between the two composition approaches. Reducible loss quickly decays to zero for source original test sets. In fact, we observe that scaling our baseline 6L-6L model by a factor of 2 _._ 5 is sufficient for ensuring that reducible loss is below 0 _._ 05 for all source original test sets. In contrast, on target original test sets, the loss decays much more slowly with model size. For comparison, to ensure that reducible loss is below 0 _._ 05 for all target original test sets, we estimate that the baseline model has to be scaled up by a factor of 11.

Because of this behavior, the value of larger models in NMT is closely tied to their evaluation sets: On source original data, due to larger scaling exponents, even moderate increases in model size are sufficient for pushing the reducible loss close to zero. Hence, beyond a few hundred million parameters, there is no benefit in increasing the model size. In contrast, for target original data, which generally have smaller scaling exponents, large models are needed to push the reducible loss to zero.

**The Effect of Training Set Construction:** In this section, we briefly examine the role of _training data_ construction on the scaling behavior. To do this, we generate two En _→_ De datasets, that were not used in the previous experiments. One fully target original and another completely source original.

To generate the target original dataset, we compile a set of German documents from the web. Documents are screened to ensure the data is not machine generated. We use a Hybrid model (with 380M parameters) (Chen et al., 2018) to back-translate (BT) these documents to English. Similarly, for the source original data, we collect human generated English documents and (forward) translate them to German using a hybrid model (with approximately 327M parameters). Both datasets provide us with approximately 2.2 billion training examples. We mimic the experimental setup of Section 2.

Note that even though these datasets are not human generated, they reflect important aspects of training large NMT models. Many modern NMT datasets are harvested from the web and as a result, are contaminated with machine generated data. Moreover, many popular data augmentation algorithms such as Back Translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), sequence level distillation (Kim & Rush, 2016) and self training (He et al., 2020) purposefully add machine generated data into the training pipeline in order to take advantage of monolingual data.

Figure 6 describes the scaling behavior for models trained on target-original data. We observe that even though larger models are successful in reducing the training loss, they are unable to improve the test loss after roughly 400M parameters. Once this size threshold is exceeded, models overfit the training data and the test loss starts to deteriorate across all of our test sets. We hypothesize that this size threshold corresponds to the capacity of the original back-translation model. This assertion suggests that in order for back-translation to be beneficial for training large models, it has to be performed with a models with comparable capacity or higher. Although quite intriguing, we leave the verification of this hypothesis to future work.

Figure 7 paints another interesting picture for the models trained on the source-original data only, implying the target side having the composition bias, expected to be simpler, dull and not rich in its content, in short - not natural looking. As experiments suggest, even our smallest models are able to achieve extremely low loss values (roughly 0 _._ 16), with an apparent overfitting pattern. We believe the same phenomenon is also related to the "data simplification" effect seeked by non-autoregressive models in NMT (Zhou et al., 2021).

## 4 EVOLUTION OF GENERATION QUALITY

We examine the effects of scaling on the output quality as measured by BLEU score[8] . For this analysis, we focus on output generated via beam search (Wu et al., 2016). For tractability purposes, we do not attempt to tune the (many) hyper-parameters of beam-search for each model. Instead, we use the configuration optimized for the baseline model (listed in Appendix J) in all the decoding tasks.

Figure 8 presents the co-evolution of BLEU score and cross-entropy loss throughout the training for all of our models. Depending on the construction of the test sets, two different empirical behaviors emerge. On target-original test sets, larger models are able to improve (lower) the test loss. These improvements in the loss are accompanied with consistent improvements (increases) in BLEU score. In fact, we observe that a simple power law can capture the relationship between BLEU score and cross-entropy loss for high-quality models.[9] In contrast, on source-original test sets, this relationship is absent; larger models consistently achieve better test losses, however, beyond a certain threshold, BLEU scores begin to deteriorate. Figures 27 and 28 exhibit that this phenomenon is not due to over-training; the BLEU score gap between large and small models is persistent throughout training.

To ensure that this observation truly reflects the generation quality of the models (as opposed to potential biases of BLEU score), we repeat our analysis with BLEURT score (Sellam et al., 2020a;b). The results are presented in Appendix K. The results show that BLEURT scores closely mirror the behavior of BLEU scores with respect to model scaling.

A careful look at the left-subplots of Figures 8 brings up another interesting trend. At similar values of the test loss, encoder-scaled models result in better generation quality compared to decoder-scaled models. This findings agrees with previous work that relied on encoder-scaling when optimizing for BLEU and inference latency (Kasai et al., 2021). Whether these differences in the effects of encoder-scaling and decoder-scaling are caused by insufficient search algorithms, or just different model fits from different architectural priors is left to future work.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND LIMITATION

In this work we have attempted to quantify the evolution of model quality as a function of model capacity for encoder-decoder NMT models. While a univariate scaling law describing the crossentropy as a function of the total number of parameters in the model is insufficient, a bivariate law treating the number of encoder and decoder parameters as separate variables adequately describes the scaling behavior of these models under various scaling strategies. We validate this behavior on a variety of language pairs and evaluation sets. Whether this behavior is intrinsic to the encoder-decoder architecture, or arising from the nature of the NMT task, requires further study.

Next, we show that this scaling behavior is highly dependent on the composition of the evaluation data, specifically on whether the source or target sentences are “original”. Our findings indicate that target-original evaluation sets continue benefiting from model scaling, while the reducible error on source-original evaluation sets quickly saturates to 0. This could be an artifact of the lack of diversity in translated text; a simpler target distribution doesn’t require much capacity to model while generating fluent or natural-looking text could benefit much more from scale.

We also study how the composition of training data affects the scaling behavior of models. When training on target-original (back-translated) text, model quality keeps improving until a point after which the trend saturates. In our study the capacity where saturation manifests first is perilously close to the capacity of the model used for back-translation, indicating that the capacity of the generative model used to generate synthetic text might have a role to play, but this requires further investigation. When training on source-original text, even low-capacity models are sufficient to reach the irreducible loss region, painting a gloomy picture for synthetic data. While we have explored these ideas in the context of machine translation, given the proliferation of generative models this problem will likely be a challenge for future practitioners training on web-scraped monolingual datasets as well. For low-resource languages, the proliferation of machine translated text is already a problem given that a significant portion of web text in these languages is machine translated.

Finally, we attempt to understand how generation quality evolves with the improvements in crossentropy resulting from model scaling. As with our previous findings, dataset composition plays a major role in determining the trends. For source-original evaluation sets, the correlation between cross-entropy and generation quality breaks down. On target-original evaluation, we observe an inverse correlation between cross-entropy and BLEU, suggesting that improved model fit results in a corresponding improvement in generation quality. The slope of this relationship is different for encoder-scaling and decoder-scaling, with encoder-scaled models performing better on BLEU than decoder-scaled models, at the same level of cross-entropy loss. Whether this is an artifact of our search strategy or the difference in architectural priors is something that requires further investigation.

Our findings suggest that scaling behavior of encoder-decoder NMT models is predictable, but the exact formulation of scaling laws might vary depending on the particular architecture or task being studied. Our empirical findings also raise concerns regarding the effect of synthetic data on model scaling and evaluation, and how proliferation of machine generated text might hamper the quality of future models trained on web-text.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous and timely investigation into the scaling laws of encoder-decoder Transformer models in the context of neural machine translation (NMT), addressing a critical gap in the literature that has largely focused on decoder-only language models. The work is well-structured, clearly motivated, and makes several compelling empirical contributions: (i) it establishes a bivariate scaling law for cross-entropy loss as a function of encoder and decoder sizes—demonstrating that total parameter count alone is insufficient for accurate prediction—(ii) it identifies differing scaling exponents for encoder versus decoder, offering practical guidance for capacity allocation, (iii) it introduces and highlights the significant impact of *composition bias*, i.e., the unnaturalness of translated vs. naturally written text, on model scaling behavior, and (iv) it uncovers a dissociation between loss reduction and BLEU score improvements depending on the directionality and naturalness of test data. These findings are supported by extensive experiments across multiple language pairs and settings, enhancing their credibility and generalizability. However, a major limitation lies in the lack of theoretical grounding for the proposed bivariate scaling law; while empirically sound, its functional form appears heuristic, and the paper would benefit from deeper analysis or justification of why this particular parametrization succeeds. Additionally, the observation that BLEU plateaus despite continued loss improvement on source-original test sets raises important questions about evaluation methodology and the validity of conventional metrics under scaling, which are noted but not fully explored. The writing is generally clear, though some technical details (e.g., exact model configurations) are deferred to an appendix, potentially limiting reproducibility for readers without access to supplementary materials. Overall, this is a strong empirical contribution that advances our understanding of NMT model scaling and exposes key challenges related to data naturalness and evaluation—issues that are likely to grow in importance as models scale further.

comments.title: Mismatch between abstract claim and formal result in generalization to translation directions
comments.quote: For test sets which were originally translated from target language to source language, both loss and BLEU score improve as model size increases. In contrast, for test sets originally translated from source language to target language, the loss improves, but the BLEU score stops improving after a certain threshold.
comments.explanation: The abstract presents a strong and novel claim about BLEU score saturation depending on test set direction. However, in the provided passage, the only related content in the introduction paraphrases this claim briefly and labels it as part of future investigation (point 3 in RQs). The experimental section (2.1) only describes measuring cross-entropy loss on various test sets, and the results discussed up to section 2.3 focus exclusively on cross-entropy, not BLEU. While the abstract states a clear dichotomy in generation quality, no BLEU results are presented in the provided text, nor is there any description of how generation was evaluated (e.g., beam search settings, detokenization). This raises concern about whether the BLEU claim is supported within the current context. It's possible that section 3 or later contains this analysis, but given that the abstract highlights it as a key finding, at least a preview or forward reference should be present. As it stands, the claim appears disconnected from the evidence presented, creating a risk that the paper overstates its results in the abstract. While forward references are acceptable, omitting any mention of BLEU evaluation methodology here is problematic for reproducibility and credibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent total parameter range implies potential OCR or reporting error
comments.quote: We train 200 such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of 50 (from 80M parameters to 4B parameters).
comments.explanation: The stated scaling factor is approximately 50, and the range goes from 80M to 4B. However, 4B / 80M = 50 exactly, which suggests consistency at first glance. But examining the scaling methods more closely raises concern: in 'Encoder Scaling', encoder depth varies from 2 to 64 while decoder is fixed at 6; each encoder layer adds ~20M, each decoder ~25M. The minimal model would have encoder=2, decoder=6 → (2×20M) + (6×25M) = 40M + 150M = 190M parameters. Even the smallest configuration described exceeds the stated '80M' lower bound. Similarly, symmetric scaling starts at 2 layers each → 2×20M + 2×25M = 90M, still above 80M. This suggests either a misreporting of the smallest model size or an error in the per-layer parameter count. However, the footnote says embeddings and softmax are excluded, which are typically substantial in Transformers, so potentially a model with very small layers (not 1024/8192) could be used for the smallest ones. But the text explicitly states that internal hyperparameters (model dimension, FFN width) are fixed across scales. Therefore, the description of the minimal model is inconsistent with the stated architecture. The error may stem from OCR (e.g., '190M' misread as '80M'), but given that the per-layer parameter additions (20M, 25M) and fixed depth minimum (2) imply at least ~90M, the '80M' is likely incorrect. This affects the credibility of the parameter scaling claim and may mislead readers about the regime studied.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in Figure Captions: Training vs Test Loss Reporting
comments.quote: Figure 6: Scaling behavior of models trained on back-translated data. Right: Increasing the model size successfully reduces the loss on the training distribution. However, on the test data (left and center) the loss increases after approximately 400M parameters.
comments.explanation: The caption claims that test loss increases after ~400M parameters across all test sets, but Figure 6 (rightmost subplot) shows training loss on 'Machine Translated Data (Training Dist)', not test loss. The left and center panels do show test loss on source- and target-original Web-Domain, but the 'increases' claimed are subtle or absent — particularly on the center (target-original) plot, where the trend appears to plateau rather than reverse. The text suggests overfitting is universal across test sets, but visually, only the source-original side shows a clear increase. Given the paper’s emphasis on the qualitative difference between source- and target-original sets, this overstatement is problematic. While Figure 7 shows clearer overfitting trends (e.g., loss rising from ~0.16 to ~0.17 in source-original), Figure 6 does not consistently support the claim. This distorts the evidence for the hypothesis that 400M is a universal threshold. The numerical threshold (400M) also doesn't align precisely with the x-axis ticks (0.2, 0.4, ...), making it hard to verify.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Power-Law Fit Between BLEU and Loss
comments.quote: In fact, we observe that a simple power law can capture the relationship between BLEU score and cross-entropy loss for high-quality models.
comments.explanation: This claim is stated confidently but without immediate justification or visual evidence in Figure 8. While Figure 8 includes dashed lines labeled 'Fitted Power-Law', these appear only on the target-original (left) subplot, yet the text implies this holds 'for high-quality models' generally. However, no quantitative measure (e.g., R²) is provided for the fit, and Appendix J (referenced for fitting details) is not in the provided context. In a paper emphasizing predictive accuracy of scaling laws, this omission is notable. Moreover, the relationship between BLEU and loss is not expected to be monotonic across models — especially in NMT, where low loss can correlate with over-smoothing. The authors correctly contrast behaviors on source- vs target-original data, but the positive assertion about a 'simple power law' is under-substantiated here. The claim may be valid, but the lack of support reduces credibility.

Similarly, 'target-original' means the target side is natural. The later discussion in the composition bias section makes it clear that naturalness on the *target* side matters for scaling benefits. Thus, the naming convention emphasizes the *origin* of the source sentence rather than the *naturalness* of the target, which is the critical variable. A reader might mistakenly think 'source-original' implies naturalness on the source, but the real impact is on the target’s linguistic quality. While the paper does clarify this distinction in the introduction, the label remains counterintuitive and could lead to confusion, especially when discussing cross-entropy on the target side. More precise terms like 'target-is-translation' and 'target-is-native' might have been clearer, but the current terms are consistently used and ultimately defined, so the issue is mitigated by context.

But the text explicitly states that internal hyperparameters (model dimension, FFN width) are fixed across scales. Therefore, the description of the minimal model is inconsistent with the stated architecture. The error may stem from OCR (e.g., '190M' misread as '80M'), but given that the per-layer parameter additions (20M, 25M) and fixed depth minimum (2) imply at least ~90M, the '80M' is likely incorrect. This affects the credibility of the parameter scaling claim and may mislead readers about the regime studied.

comments.title: Decoder vs encoder parameter asymmetry not fully accounted for in symmetric scaling
comments.quote: Symmetric Scaling: increasing decoder and encoder layers together (from 2 to 64), i.e. the number of Transformer Blocks in the encoder and decoder being equal.
comments.explanation: The authors define symmetric scaling as equal numbers of encoder and decoder layers. However, each decoder layer contributes ~25M parameters vs ~20M for encoder, so equal depth does not imply symmetric parameter growth. This is a reasonable experimental design choice, but the term 'symmetric scaling' could mislead a reader into thinking parameter allocation is symmetric, when it is not. The authors correctly note the parameter asymmetry earlier, so technically they are not lying. However, in the context of studying bivariate scaling laws where Ne and Nd are separate variables, calling a depth-equal setup 'symmetric' risks conflating architectural symmetry with parameter symmetry. A more precise term might be 'depth-symmetric scaling'. But given that the authors clearly define it operationally and the results distinguish Ne and Nd, the potential confusion is minor and resolvable from context. The choice is valid and the terminology is not incorrect, just imprecise.

comments.title: Missing formula for proposed scaling law
comments.quote: Then, our proposed scaling law has the form
comments.explanation: Immediately after this sentence, the paper claims to present a formula, but no equation is visible in the provided text—likely due to formatting or OCR failure. From the surrounding context, particularly the description of parameters α, pe, pd, and L∞, and the later reference to Eq. (1), we infer that the intended formula is something like L̂(Ne, Nd) = α Ne^(-pe) Nd^(-pd) + L∞, or possibly a sum of power laws. This is strongly supported by the bivariate nature of the claim and the exponents pe and pd. However, the absence of the equation—especially at such a critical point—makes the paper underspecified at this juncture. A reader cannot verify the functional form or reproduce the fitting procedure without it. While the equation likely appears elsewhere (e.g., in the figure captions or later text), its omission here is a significant issue in terms of reproducibility and clarity. Given that the authors claim to 'present a new scaling law', the absence of its mathematical form is a serious flaw in this section—though we assume it exists in the full paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'composition bias'
comments.quote: We observe that naturalness of the source/target side data affect scaling behavior... We observe that natural text on the target side enjoys scaling...
comments.explanation: The term 'composition bias' is defined in the summary as 'deviation from naturally generated text... via machine generated or human translated text'. However, in practice, the authors use it primarily in the context of target-side naturalness: whether the target text is 'natural' (target-original) or 'translationese' (source-original). The focus is on target-side effects, yet the definition includes both source and target. This risks ambiguity: does composition bias refer to source, target, or both? The passage suggests it's mostly about target-side naturalness, and the test sets are classified by whether the source or target was original. Given the emphasis on target-side artifacts like translationese, and the observation that BLEU plateaus only on source-original (non-natural target) data, the analysis is target-centric. However, the term 'composition bias' as initially defined could mislead a reader into thinking both sides are equally considered, when the key findings hinge on target-side naturalness. This is a framing issue: the definition is slightly overbroad relative to its application.

comments.title: Incorrect Formula in Optimal Scaling Proposition
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expressions for $ N_e^* $ and $ N_d^* $ in Proposition 1 appear nonsensical: they seem to suggest that optimal encoder/decoder sizes are linear in $ B $ but with coefficients involving only exponents $ p_e $ and $ p_d $ summed in a way that doesn't match dimensional expectations. Specifically, the forms _pep_ + _epd[B] and _pep_ + _dpd[B] look like OCR corruption. Given the context, the intended formula is almost certainly $ N_e^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e + p_d} B $ and $ N_d^* = \frac{p_d}{p_e + p_d} B $, which is the standard result for optimizing a bivariate power law $ L \propto N_e^{-p_e} N_d^{-p_d} $ under a total parameter budget $ B = N_e + N_d $. This is derived by minimizing $ \alpha N_e^{-p_e} N_d^{-p_d} $ subject to $ N_e + N_d = B $ using Lagrange multipliers, leading to the ratio $ N_d / N_e = p_d / p_e $, hence the proportional allocation. The text even claims that when $ N_d / N_e = p_d / p_e $, optimal scaling is achieved — which aligns with the correct derivation — suggesting that the displayed equations are merely garbled by OCR. However, since this is a formal proposition and its proof is deferred to an appendix, the current rendering is more than a formatting issue: a reader cannot verify or use the result without guessing the correction. The surrounding explanation supports the intent, but the equation as written is mathematically incoherent and would mislead a reader relying solely on this section.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Text and Figure Labels in Figure 5
comments.quote: Figure 5 presents the results evaluated on in-domain held-out data. Similar to the En _→_ De case, our proposed functional form is able to closely capture the scaling behavior of these models.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Figure 5' presenting results for German → English, Chinese → English, and English → Chinese, but the embedded picture text labeled as 'Figure 5' only shows three plots without clear titles matching these language pairs. Instead, it shows labels like 'Task: De →En', 'Task: En →Zh', 'Task: Zh →En', which do match the mentioned tasks. However, the picture text placement is ambiguous — it appears immediately after Figure 4's picture text and before any caption identifying it as Figure 5. Given that Figure 4's picture already includes a plot labeled 'Randomly sampled English → German', and the next block shows three plots with different tasks, it's plausible that this is Figure 5. But the absence of a clear 'Figure 5' label in the text or in the picture block creates ambiguity. Crucially, the R² values shown (99.8%, 99.8%, 99.7%) align with the claim of strong fit across language pairs. While the content itself is consistent with the claim, the labeling is underspecified due to OCR extraction failure — the logical flow suggests it's Figure 5, but without a definitive header, a careful reader might question whether this visualization corresponds to the symmetric/random models (Figure 3/4) or the cross-lingual analysis. The context strongly implies it’s Figure 5, but the lack of explicit identification is a presentation flaw that could cause confusion in a skim.

comments.title: Ambiguous Meaning of 'R [2]' in Figure Captions
comments.quote: Web Domain 1 (src orig),  R [2] : 99.4, ‖ L  − L ‖∞: 0.006
comments.explanation: The notation 'R [2]' appears repeatedly in figure captions and is used to report values like 99.4, 99.8, etc. From context, it clearly refers to R² (coefficient of determination), and the paper even parenthetically defines it earlier as 'R-squared (100 × explained variance / total variance)' in Figure 1's caption. However, the typographical rendering 'R [2]' instead of 'R²' or 'R^2' is due to OCR failure. This could mislead a reader unfamiliar with statistical notation — especially since '[2]' might be interpreted as a footnote or subscript. The surrounding explanation resolves the ambiguity for an experienced reader, but for accessibility and clarity, this should be 'R²'. Since it's repeated across all figures and not clarified in the caption of Figure 2 onward (only in Figure 1), it becomes a recurring ambiguity. While not a technical error, it's a flaw in communication that could frustrate or confuse readers, particularly students or those from non-statistical backgrounds. Because the meaning is recoverable from context and the initial definition, and because the value scale (e.g. 99.4) is typical for R² percentages, this does not rise to a major issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Natural' in Test Set Descriptions
comments.quote: On the other hand, improvements on cross-entropy saturate (or reaches the irreducible error region) on source side natural evaluation sets even for moderately-sized models.
comments.explanation: This sentence from the Introduction appears to contradict the defined terminology. According to the PAPER SUMMARY, 'target-original' test sets contain natural target-side text, while 'source-original' contain translationese on the target side. Yet here, the phrase 'source side natural evaluation sets' is used. This could be misread as a test set where the source is natural — which all are — but the discussion is clearly about the *target-side naturalness*. Earlier in the Introduction, the authors correctly state: 'Source-original data has translated text on the target side... while target-original data requires the prediction of more complex natural text on the target side.' So when they later say 'source side natural', they likely mean 'test sets where the *source* is natural' — but this is trivially true of all real test sets. The intended meaning almost certainly refers to *target-original* sets, where the target text is natural. This appears to be a slip in phrasing rather than a conceptual error. The rest of the paper uses the correct 'target-original' vs 'source-original' distinction. Still, this particular sentence risks misalignment: a reader might think the paper claims that models fail to scale on *source-natural* data — which is not the point. The key claim is that scaling helps when predicting *natural target* text (target-original), not translationese (source-original). The ambiguity here could lead to misinterpretation of the core finding.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Proportional Scaling Optimality
comments.quote: Inspection of the functional form of Eq. (1) suggests that as long as $ N_d/N_e $ is fixed as the model scales (i.e. encoder and decoder grow proportionally together), the optimal scaling exponent, ($ p_e + p_d $), can be achieved, albeit with a potentially sub-optimal multiplicative constant, $ \alpha^{\#} $.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this passage appears to suggest that fixing $ N_d/N_e $ during scaling yields the optimal exponent $ p_e + p_d $, which aligns with Proposition 1. The claim that the exponent is preserved under proportional scaling is mathematically sound: if $ N_d = r N_e $, then $ L \propto N_e^{-p_e} N_d^{-p_d} = N_e^{-(p_e + p_d)} r^{-p_d} $, so total parameter count $ N \sim N_e $ gives power law $ N^{-(p_e + p_d)} $, matching the optimal exponent. The use of a mysterious $ \alpha^{\#} $ instead of $ \alpha^* $ initially raised concern—could this be a typo or an unexplained deviation from the earlier definition of $ \alpha^* $? But the text correctly notes that only the multiplicative constant may be sub-optimal, not the exponent. However, the notation $ \alpha^{\#} $ is not defined anywhere, and contradicts the established symbol $ \alpha^* $ from the PAPER SUMMARY. Given that $ \alpha^* $ is precisely defined as the constant under optimal allocation, and that any fixed ratio $ N_d/N_e $ leads to a constant (but not necessarily optimal) prefactor, the use of a different symbol $ \alpha^{\#} $ is unnecessary and confusing. A clear explanation would simply state that $ \alpha $ in the resulting power law depends on the chosen ratio and is maximized (i.e., loss minimized) when the ratio matches $ N_d^*/N_e^* $. The ad hoc symbol $ \alpha^{\#} $ adds ambiguity and lacks justification. While the core idea is correct, the notation undermines clarity without adding value.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation for Total Parameters in Figure Captions
comments.quote: Total Non-Embeddings Parameters 1e9
comments.explanation: In multiple figure captions and axis labels (e.g., Figure 5), the x-axis is labeled 'Total Non-Embeddings Parameters 1e9', which contains a grammatical and notational inconsistency: 'Non-Embeddings' should be 'Non-Embedding' (as in other sections). More importantly, the paper defines $ N_e $ and $ N_d $ as non-embedding encoder and decoder parameters, and total model size as $ N = N_e + N_d $. The axes clearly intend to display $ N $ in units of 1e9. However, the label 'Total Non-Embeddings Parameters 1e9' is redundant and awkward—'Total Number of Parameters (Excluding Embeddings) [1e9]' is used elsewhere (e.g., Figure 1) and is clearer. While this is likely a formatting or OCR artifact (note: 'Non-Embeddings' vs 'Non-Embedding'), the inconsistency across figures could confuse a careful reader about whether different quantities are being plotted. However, context resolves this: the values match the expected total $ N_e + N_d $, and the definition is consistent elsewhere. The label does not introduce a mathematical error, but reflects a lack of typographic consistency. Given that OCR may have introduced the plural 's', this is likely not an authorial error. Still, in final form, such inconsistencies should be cleaned for professionalism.

comments.title: Overstatement of Correlation in Figure 4 Caption
comments.quote: The predictions are more than 99% 99% correlated with the empirical test loss values.
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 4 states that predictions are 'more than 99% 99% correlated', which appears to be a duplication error (possibly from OCR or copy-paste). The figure panel shows 'Corr(L, L̂) : 0.993' and '0.999', which are 99.3% and 99.9% correlation—correctly over 99%. So the intent is accurate: the correlation exceeds 99%. The phrase 'more than 99% 99%' is clearly a typo, likely intended as 'more than 99%'. While the numerical results support the claim, the phrasing is awkward and could momentarily confuse a reader into thinking a 198% correlation is claimed. However, the actual correlation values are reported correctly in the figure, and the duplicate '99%' is likely a production error. Since the underlying data and metrics are correct, and the error is superficial, it does not affect the validity of the claim. This is best treated as a copyediting issue.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Claimed and Formal Definition of Optimal Allocation in Proposition 1 Restatement
comments.quote: Then, the optimal encoder / decoder sizes (denoted respectively by Ne[∗][and][N] d[ ∗][)][are][N] e[ ∗]_[=] _pep_ + _epd[B][and][N] d[ ∗]_[=] _pep_ + _dpd[B.]
comments.explanation: In the restatement of Proposition 1, the formulas for $ N_e^* $ and $ N_d^* $ are garbled due to OCR or formatting failure. The intended formulas, per the PAPER SUMMARY, are $ N_e^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e + p_d} B $ and $ N_d^* = \frac{p_d}{p_e + p_d} B $. The text shows '_pep_ + _epd[B]' which is clearly meant to be $ \frac{p_e}{p_e + p_d} B $, but the fraction structure is lost. Similarly, '_pep_ + _dpd[B]' likely means $ \frac{p_d}{p_e + p_d} B $. The surrounding discussion refers correctly to the ratio $ N_d/N_e = p_d/p_e $, and the later derivation of $ \alpha^* $ matches the correct formula. Thus, the OCR has corrupted the mathematical notation, but the intended meaning is recoverable from context and matches the formal definitions. A reader might initially worry that the allocation is claimed to be $ p_e p + e p_d B $ (nonsensical), but the rest of the discussion confirms the correct interpretation. This is not an author error but a transcription failure. However, since the passage is presented as extracted text, and the error would mislead a reader who cannot infer the correct form, it qualifies as a moderate issue in this context. In the original paper, these formulas would be properly typeset.

comments.title: Ambiguous Role of $ \sigma $ in Prediction Plots Without Definition
comments.quote: L  ± 2 σ
comments.explanation: The plots (e.g., Figure 3) include bands labeled 'L ± 2σ', implying uncertainty or variance in predictions or measurements. The text mentions in Figure 2 caption: 'We anticipate some fluctuations around the predicted trend (σ = 0.003) caused by the randomness in the training pipeline (see Appendix E).' However, in the out-of-sample figures (3 and 4), σ is not redefined. While Appendix E (not shown) is cited, the immediate text does not clarify whether σ represents model variance, training noise, or prediction uncertainty. In Figure 3, the 'Different Seeds' points suggest σ quantifies training stochasticity. But without explicit linkage, a reader might misinterpret the band as a confidence interval from the scaling law fit, rather than Monte Carlo variation from training randomness. The authors do mention the seed variation, but the connection between 'σ' in the figure and 'training randomness' is underspecified in the figure captions themselves. This is a common practice in ML papers, but for a rigorous reviewer, the failure to specify what σ quantifies in each figure (even if deferred to an appendix) slightly undermines reproducibility. However, since the convention is well-established and the appendix is referenced, this is a mild shortcoming.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Composition Bias Definition in Passage
comments.quote: composition bias, expected to be simpler, dull and not rich in its content, in short - not natural looking
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the phrase 'composition bias, expected to be simpler, dull and not rich in its content' incorrectly attributes stylistic properties directly to the concept of composition bias. Composition bias was formally defined earlier as a data artifact arising from the presence of machine-translated or human-translated content in training/test sets, not as a style per se. However, upon closer reading, this passage appears to be describing the *consequence* of composition bias on the target side — specifically, that when the target side is machine-translated (as in source-original data), it tends to be stylistically 'simpler' and 'duller'. This aligns with the paper’s broader claim that target-side naturalness affects scaling. The issue is not a definitional error but a momentary conflation of cause and effect. A more careful phrasing would distinguish the bias (a data condition) from its linguistic effect (reduced complexity). Still, given the surrounding context, a careful reader is unlikely to be misled long-term.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in Figure Captions: Training vs Test Loss Reporting
comments.quote: Figure 6: Scaling behavior of models trained on back-translated data. Right: Increasing the model size successfully reduces the loss on the training distribution. However, on the test data (left and center) the loss increases after approximately 400M parameters.
comments.explanation: The caption claims that test loss increases after ~400M parameters across all test sets, but Figure 6 (rightmost subplot) shows training loss on 'Machine Translated Data (Training Dist)', not test loss. The left and center panels do show test loss on source- and target-original Web-Domain, but the 'increases' claimed are subtle or absent — particularly on the center (target-original) plot, where the trend appears to plateau rather than reverse. The text suggests overfitting is universal across test sets, but visually, only the source-original side shows a clear increase. Given the paper’s emphasis on the qualitative difference between source- and target-original sets, this overstatement is problematic. While Figure 7 shows clearer overfitting trends (e.g., loss rising from ~0.16 to ~0.17 in source-original), Figure 6 does not consistently support the claim. This distorts the evidence for the hypothesis that 400M is a universal threshold. The numerical threshold (400M) also doesn't align precisely with the x-axis ticks (0.2, 0.4, ...), making it hard to verify.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Power-Law Fit Between BLEU and Loss
comments.quote: In fact, we observe that a simple power law can capture the relationship between BLEU score and cross-entropy loss for high-quality models.
comments.explanation: This claim is stated confidently but without immediate justification or visual evidence in Figure 8. While Figure 8 includes dashed lines labeled 'Fitted Power-Law', these appear only on the target-original (left) subplot, yet the text implies this holds 'for high-quality models' generally. However, no quantitative measure (e.g., R²) is provided for the fit, and Appendix J (referenced for fitting details) is not in the provided context. In a paper emphasizing predictive accuracy of scaling laws, this omission is notable. Moreover, the relationship between BLEU and loss is not expected to be monotonic across models — especially in NMT, where low loss can correlate with over-smoothing. The authors correctly contrast behaviors on source- vs target-original data, but the positive assertion about a 'simple power law' is under-substantiated here. The claim may be valid, but the lack of support reduces credibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Model Size' in Scaling Descriptions
comments.quote: models overfit the training data and the test loss starts to deteriorate across all of our test sets. We hypothesize that this size threshold corresponds to the capacity of the original back-translation model.
comments.explanation: The term 'model size' is used here to refer to the size of the trained NMT model, but the hypothesis links this to the 'capacity of the original back-translation model' — a different model used to generate the training data. While conceptually plausible, the passage fails to clarify whether the back-translation model was actually scaled or held fixed. The full paper context assumes back-translation uses a 'strong baseline MT system', but does not specify its size. If the threshold (~400M) corresponds to the capacity of that fixed baseline, then the claim makes sense. But without confirming that the baseline was ~400M, the hypothesis feels speculative. The authors do rightly label it as a hypothesis to be tested later, which mitigates the issue. Still, stating the baseline model’s size (even approximately) would strengthen the argument.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Forward Translated Data' Labeling in Figure 7
comments.quote: Figure 7: Scaling behavior of models trained on forward translated data. Left / center: early stopping test loss on Web-Domain. Right: loss at the end of the training for a subset of the training data.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'forward translated data' is introduced here without prior definition. From context, it appears to mean: source-side is human-written, target-side is machine-translated — i.e., source-original training data. This was defined earlier in the full context, but this section doesn't reiterate it. For a reader skimming the figures, 'forward translated' might be misinterpreted as referring to the inference direction. The rightmost subplot shows very low training loss (~0.1), consistent with model overfitting on synthetic translationese, but without a clear definition, the link between 'forward translation' and source-original composition bias is not transparent. Given that the paper’s core argument rests on this distinction, a brief definitional reminder would help prevent misinterpretation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'reducible error saturates to 0'
comments.quote: the reducible error on source-original evaluation sets quickly saturates to 0
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems to suggest that the reducible loss (i.e., the difference between total loss and irreducible loss) goes to zero, implying perfect performance. This would be a strong claim. However, in the context of scaling laws, 'reducible error' is often interpreted as L - L∞, and the paper elsewhere mentions that source-original data saturates quickly with reducible loss < 0.05 after modest scaling. Thus, the phrase 'saturates to 0' is likely intended to mean 'approaches zero' or 'becomes negligible', not that it reaches exactly zero. Given the paper’s earlier quantitative claims (e.g., loss threshold of 0.05), this appears to be an imprecise but contextually resolvable simplification. Still, a careful reader might initially infer a stronger result than is supported, especially since no limit or approximation is formally qualified.

comments.title: Inconsistent correlation metric: 'Corr(L, L)' in Figure 4 caption
comments.quote: Corr( L , L ) : 0.993
comments.explanation: The notation 'Corr(L, L)' is used in Figure 4 to describe a correlation value, but this is formally degenerate: the correlation of a variable with itself is always 1. This appears in both the picture text and the caption. However, from the context — especially the surrounding figures using R² and the mention of 'predictions vs empirical' — it's clear that 'Corr(L, L)' is a typo and should be 'Corr(L_pred, L_emp)' or similar. Given that the associated R² values are 99.3% and 99.9%, a correlation near 0.995–0.999 is plausible. This is likely an OCR or typesetting error, but since the symbol appears twice and matches the magnitude expected from R² ≈ 0.99, it’s not a critical flaw. Still, without the figure, a reader might be briefly confused.

comments.title: Overstatement of BLEU correlation direction
comments.quote: we observe an inverse correlation between cross-entropy and BLEU
comments.explanation: The phrase 'inverse correlation' is technically correct — cross-entropy (loss) decreases as BLEU increases — but in machine learning, it is conventional to say there is a 'positive correlation between model improvement and BLEU', or 'negative correlation between loss and BLEU'. The term 'inverse correlation' is vague and could mislead a reader into thinking the relationship is not monotonic or is causal in the opposite direction. However, the next sentence clarifies the directionality ('improved model fit results in corresponding improvement'), so the intent is clear. This is more of a linguistic imprecision than a logical error, but in a scientific context where 'inverse' might be interpreted as 1/x or non-linear, it risks confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguity in claim about training on target-original text
comments.quote: When training on target-original (back-translated) text, model quality keeps improving until a point after which the trend saturates. In our study the capacity where saturation manifests first is perilously close to the capacity of the model used for back-translation
comments.explanation: This suggests a causal link between back-translation model capacity and saturation during downstream training, which is a significant claim. However, the passage does not clarify whether the back-translation model was held fixed across experiments or varied with model size. If the BT model was small (e.g., 400M), and larger downstream models (>400M) overfit, that supports their hypothesis. But without stating the BT model size explicitly here (though possibly in appendices), a reader cannot fully evaluate the strength of this claim. The summary later says: 'models larger than ~400M parameters overfit if back-translation is performed with smaller-capacity models', which resolves the issue retroactively. Still, in isolation, the passage underspecifies a key experimental detail that affects the validity of the causal interpretation.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in 'L  ± 2 σ' formatting
comments.quote: L  ± 2 σ
comments.explanation: Throughout the figure texts, the uncertainty band is labeled 'L  ± 2 σ', which likely means 'predicted loss ± 2 standard deviations'. The space in 'L  ±' and inconsistent spacing may stem from OCR issues. However, in all cases it appears adjacent to predicted or fitted lines, and the magnitude of σ (e.g., 0.003) is consistent with the scale of fluctuations described in Appendix E. While typographically awkward, the intent is clear: to represent prediction uncertainty. No technical error is present, just a formatting artifact. Hence, no flag is warranted beyond noting it as a possible OCR quirk.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing about 'generation quality breaks down'
comments.quote: the correlation between cross-entropy and generation quality breaks down
comments.explanation: This is a strong claim: that the usual proxy relationship between training loss and evaluation metrics fails. The context supports this — later they note BLEU and BLEURT deteriorate after a threshold despite loss improvements. However, 'breaks down' could be interpreted as 'becomes uncorrelated' or 'becomes negative'. A more precise term would be 'inverts' or 'decouples'. Still, the subsequent sentence clarifies the nature of the breakdown by contrasting it with the target-original case, making the intended meaning recoverable. This phrasing is common in ML literature, so while slightly imprecise, it's defensible.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is a clear, well-motivated contribution to the literature on neural scaling laws, successfully extending the discourse from decoder-only language models to the more complex, conditional setting of encoder-decoder architectures. The writing is precise, the research questions are well-defined, and the identification of "composition bias" as a mediator of scaling behavior provides a sophisticated, non-trivial insight that distinguishes the paper from standard empirical benchmarking. However, the most significant risk in the subsequent sections will be the robustness of the proposed bivariate scaling formula; as a reviewer, I would look for rigorous justification of why a bivariate model is necessary compared to more parsimonious or alternative parameterizations of FLOPs or depth/width ratios. Furthermore, the paper’s conclusion—that model scaling does not always improve BLEU scores due to translation artifacts—is potentially disruptive; the authors must ensure that their distinction between "source-original" and "target-original" data is sufficiently granular to rule out simple data quality issues or alignment artifacts that might otherwise explain the observed performance saturation.

comments.title: Potentially circular notation in optimal scaling derivation
comments.quote: and N_{d}^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e+p_d} B
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the proposition for optimal allocation of encoder/decoder capacity is stated as $N_{e}^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e+p_d} B$ and $N_{d}^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e+p_d} B$. Given that $B$ represents the *total* number of parameters ($B = N_e + N_d$), it is mathematically impossible for both $N_e^*$ and $N_d^*$ to be proportional to $p_e / (p_e + p_d)$. Summing these would result in $(p_e / (p_e + p_d)) * B + (p_e / (p_e + p_d)) * B = \frac{2p_e}{p_e+p_d} B$, which would only equal $B$ if $p_e = p_d$. The derivation likely contains a typo where the coefficients were swapped for the decoder to ensure the sum equals $B$ (i.e., $N_d^* = \frac{p_d}{p_e+p_d} B$).

comments.title: Ambiguous variable naming in optimal scaling proposition
comments.quote: N_{e}^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e+p_d} B
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction shows 'N_{e}^* = \frac{p_e}{p_e+p_d} B' and 'N_{d}^* = \frac{pe}{p_e+p_d} B'. Beyond the likely index error (using 'p_e' in the numerator of the decoder allocation), the notation is garbled (e.g., 'p_e p_ + e p_ d'). While the intent is clear from the context of optimizing a power law, the formal definition is corrupted to the point where a reader cannot verify the derivation without correcting the OCR artifacts.

comments.title: Missing Display Equation for Scaling Law
comments.quote:
Then, our proposed scaling law has the form

where $\{ \alpha, p_e, p_d, L_\infty \}$ are test set specific (fitted) parameters.
comments.explanation: The text references a formula for the scaling law but provides the layout for the equation without the actual mathematical expression for $L(N_e, N_d)$. Specifically, there is a visible gap where the LaTeX equation should be rendered. While the variables are explained, the absence of the functional form makes it impossible to verify the claims regarding the bivariate nature of the scaling law or the relationship between variables.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Translationese' Definition
comments.quote: Source-original data has translated text on the target side, which contains several artifacts of “translationese” that distinguish it from text originally written in that language... while target-original data requires the prediction of more complex natural text on the target side.
comments.explanation: The authors posit that source-original data is 'translationese' (which is widely accepted), but then label target-original as requiring the prediction of 'natural text'. While this is the authors' definition for the study, it blurs the line between the *source text's* naturalness and the *target-side* quality. It could be interpreted as implying that target-original data has no translationese, whereas in reality, any human translation involves some degree of source-language influence. The phrasing is slightly imprecise but standard for the specific domain of machine translation evaluation papers.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter growth claims
comments.quote: Each encoder layer adds approximately 20M parameters to the model while each decoder layer adds around 25M parameters. We train 200 such models which scale the model size by approximately a factor of 50 (from 80M parameters to 4B parameters).
comments.explanation: If the smallest models (presumably 2-layer encoders/decoders) start at 80M, and we scale up to 64 layers (an increase of 60+ layers), a simple calculation using these layer parameter additions suggests the total size would be approximately 80M + 60*(20M+25M) = 2.78B parameters. The paper claims scaling up to 4B parameters. While there may be non-linearities in the embedding/projection dimensions or shared parameters not fully accounted for in this simple additive heuristic, the discrepancy between the stated incremental cost and the final total parameter count is notable for a study predicated on empirical scaling law exactness.

comments.title: OCR-induced garbling of Proposition 1 formulas
comments.quote: optimal encoder / decoder sizes (denoted respectively by Ne[∗][and][N] d[ ∗][)][are][N] e[ ∗]_[=] _pep_ + _epd[B][and][N] d[ ∗]_[=] _pep_ + _dpd[B.][In][addition,]
comments.explanation: The OCR has severely corrupted the mathematical expressions for optimal encoder/decoder sizing. While I can infer the likely intent from standard optimization (constrained optimization of $L(N_e, N_d) = \alpha N_e^{-p_e} N_d^{-p_d} + L_{\infty}$ subject to $N_e + N_d = B$), the text is physically unreadable as written. The symbols $p_e$ and $p_d$ are interleaved with brackets and extraneous characters like 'p' and 'e' that appear to be noise from the OCR process. This renders a key mathematical result of the paper inaccessible without reference to the original source.

comments.title: Inconsistent OCR of the optimal scaling constant
